title: Urine on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15–45 minutes (fresh) or up to 2 hours (aged odor present)
stainChemistry: Urine is urea + uric-acid crystals + urochrome (a yellow pigment derived from hemoglobin breakdown). Fresh urine is mildly acidic (pH 4.5–6.5) and lifts fast. Aged urine is the real problem: bacteria metabolize the urea into ammonia, raising pH above 7, which precipitates uric-acid crystals AND produces the characteristic sharp odor. Cotton is cellulose — chemically durable, tolerates enzymes, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash. The treatment has to clear both the stain AND the bacteria; otherwise the smell comes back.
whyThisWorks: Enzyme first to digest the urea + uric acid protein chain. Acid-side pass (acetic acid / white vinegar in home) dissolves residual uric-acid crystals and neutralizes the alkaline ammonia that builds under bacterial action. Peroxide oxidizes the urochrome pigment to colorless. NSD flush removes all chemistry. Hot wash kills any surviving bacteria — without the hot wash the odor will return in a few hours even if the visible stain is gone.
defaultAssumption: Fresh urine on cotton is an easy win; aged (>24 hours) is a two-pass job because the bacterial-odor loop has to be broken. Pet urine on a mattress or upholstery escalates earlier — the padding traps bacteria the home chemistry can't reach.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Press a clean towel under from the top and blot hard. Hydraulic flow pushes urea and uric-acid crystals out the way they came in. Cold only — warm water accelerates bacterial metabolism and locks the odor in before chemistry has a chance. Continue until the towel comes up clear or pale.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease digestant) or enzyme pre-soak dissolved in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply the Protein Spotter directly. The enzyme window is warm, not hot — 30–38°C / 85–100°F (above 50°C the protease denatures). Submerge the stain or keep it wet under a damp towel. Brush gently with a soft bristle brush after the first 5 minutes. Protease hydrolyzes the peptide backbone of urea and cleaves the protein matrix that holds uric acid + urochrome in place.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Dilute acetic acid (pro: 5% aqueous or Chemspec tannin-acid spotter)
spottingProtocol.instruction: After the enzyme pass, flush with an acid-side rinse. The acid dissolves residual uric-acid crystals that the enzyme loosens but doesn't fully clear, and neutralizes alkaline ammonia from bacterial breakdown. Work from the back of the fabric.

For any residual yellow urochrome shadow: apply 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Peroxide oxidizes the urochrome to colorless. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. One oxidizer at a time; NSD-flush between if a second pass is needed.
Flush with NSD to clear all enzyme, acid, and peroxide residue. Any oxidizer left in the fiber yellows cotton over time under dryer heat.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine (hot cycle, 60°C+), sunlight-drying line if available
spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label permits — heat is what kills the remaining odor-causing bacteria. Cold wash leaves them alive and the smell returns. Dry in direct sunlight when possible; UV has an antimicrobial effect and is the cleanest odor finisher for urine.

steps: 1. Cold flush from the back — fresh urine clears fast.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B / protease) in warm water (30–38°C). Dwell 15–20 min.
3. Acid rinse (acetic acid 5% aqueous) — dissolves residual uric-acid, neutralizes ammonia.
4. 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop on residual urochrome. One pass. NSD-flush before any second pass.
5. NSD flush to clear all chemistry before heat.
6. Hot wash per care label + sunlight dry — heat kills bacteria, UV finishes the odor.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or protease digestant)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush; warm (30–38°C) for enzyme; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Warm water only in the enzyme window; above 50°C the protease denatures
Never warm-flush fresh urine — bacterial growth is the odor engine
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between
Flush all chemistry before heat — residual oxidizer yellows cotton

homeSolutions.instruction: Flush the spot with cold water from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — warm water feeds the bacteria that cause the smell. Blot hard with a clean white towel from underneath. On fresh accidents most of the stain lifts here.

Use an enzyme laundry pretreat — OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or a pet-specific enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle, Rocco & Roxie). Dissolve powder in WARM water per the label (warm, not hot — hot water kills the enzymes). Apply to the stain. Let it sit 15–20 minutes. The enzymes digest the urea and uric-acid protein that hold the odor in.

Mix equal parts white vinegar and cool water. Sponge onto the stain or soak the area for 5 minutes. Vinegar dissolves residual uric-acid crystals the enzyme leaves behind AND neutralizes the ammonia smell. This step is the reason old urine stops smelling after treatment.
If a yellow or brown shadow remains, dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide onto the spot. Let it bubble 3–5 minutes, then blot. Skip this step on dark or dyed cotton — peroxide lightens dye. Rinse cool.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia, chlorine bleach, or vinegar at the same time — one at a time with a rinse between.

homeSolutions.instruction: Flush cool water through the back of the fabric until the blotting towel runs clean. No peroxide, vinegar, or detergent residue should remain before the wash step.

Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. The hot wash is non-negotiable for urine — it's what kills the remaining odor bacteria. Cold wash leaves them alive and the smell comes back overnight. Dry outside in direct sunlight when possible; UV kills anything the wash missed.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate If the Smell Comes Back
homeSolutions.instruction: If the stain is gone but the odor returns after drying, the bacteria are deeper than home treatment can reach. Repeat the full cycle once. If it still returns, escalate to a professional — for a mattress or upholstery this is almost always the call. Tell them: "Urine on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + vinegar + 3% hydrogen peroxide + hot wash. [Odor returned after drying / stain gone.]"

steps: 1. Cold water flush from the back — never warm while the stain is fresh.
2. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Nature's Miracle), dissolved in warm-not-hot water. Dwell 15–20 min.
3. Equal-parts white vinegar + cool water — dissolves uric-acid crystals, neutralizes ammonia.
4. 3% hydrogen peroxide on residual yellow shadow. Skip on dark cotton. One oxidizer at a time.
6. Hot wash + sunlight dry — heat kills odor bacteria.
7. If odor returns after drying: repeat once, or escalate to a pro for mattress/upholstery.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz) or pet-specific enzyme cleaner (Nature's Miracle, Rocco & Roxie)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the odor keeps returning after two full cycles, if the urine's soaked into a mattress/upholstery padding, or if the stain's been through a hot wash already without enzyme treatment first. Call a pro.
warnings: Never warm-water-flush fresh urine — bacterial growth sets the odor in permanently
Never mix hydrogen peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fumes
Skip peroxide on dark/dyed cotton — test an inside seam first
The hot wash is non-negotiable for urine — cold wash leaves bacteria alive

neverDo: Never warm-flush fresh urine — accelerates bacterial growth and locks odor in
Never layer peroxide and ammonia and chlorine bleach — toxic fume risk
Never skip the hot wash on urine — cold wash = bacteria survive and odor returns
Never dry in a hot machine dryer with residual chemistry in the fiber — yellows cotton
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, acid-side spotters, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide
Mattress / upholstery urine on cotton-blend padding escalates to pro — bacteria trap in padding

materialWarnings: Cold flush only while fresh — warm water breeds bacteria, sets odor
Hot wash non-negotiable — kills remaining bacteria
Peroxide on whites/lights; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Urine on mattress / upholstery padding, odor returns after two full home cycles, or stain has already been machine-dried without enzyme treatment.
escalation.whatToTell: "Urine on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + vinegar + 3% hydrogen peroxide + hot wash. [Odor returned after drying / stain gone.]"
escalation.specialistType: professional cleaner (carpet / upholstery specialist if padding involved)

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, or days/weeks?
Is the cotton garment, mattress cover, bedding, or upholstery?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer already?
Pet or human urine? (pet urine tends to have higher uric acid, deeper penetration)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Odor-persistence is the real difficulty gate, not the visible stain. Mattress + urine + any age = pro. Fresh on garment = easy DIY.
